nativeRange.habitat: Lower-intertidal and shallow subtidal reef and rubble zones, where it hides among coral overhangs, large boulders, and rock crevices and emerges at night to forage. Often found on muddy or rocky substrate with weed and algae cover.

decorPreferences: well-established live rock with caves and overhangs
rubble zones and crevices for daytime hiding
mature reef aquascape with diverse macroalgae and sponges to use as cover
subdued or moderate lighting that supports nocturnal foraging

diet.notes: Omnivorous scavenger documented eating detritus, algae, small invertebrates, carrion, mollusks, worms, ascidians, and occasionally small or weakened sea stars. In aquariums it generally accepts meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, chopped clam, fish, or shrimp, plus algae wafers and other prepared foods. Hungry individuals are more likely to nip soft corals or harass slow-moving tankmates, so adults are typically target-fed 2–4 times per week (juveniles more often) to keep them out of trouble.

goodWith: most peaceful to semi-aggressive reef fish too large to be caught
well-established mixed reef setups with abundant hiding places
robust SPS-dominant systems where frag-theft is less consequential
other peaceful cleanup-crew species at adequate spacing
avoidWith: conspecifics in small to mid-size tanks (territorial)
prized show corals or expensive soft-coral frags (frag-theft risk)
small, slow-moving shrimp and ornamental snails (potential prey if hungry)
any system being dosed with copper-based medications

summary: An Indo-Pacific spider crab famous for fastening sponges, algae, and even coral frags to its hooked carapace as living camouflage. Generally considered reef-safe with caution because it may snip soft-coral pieces to wear them.
careNotes: The Decorator Crab's most distinctive trait is its carapace and legs, which are covered in tiny hooked bristles that work like Velcro, allowing it to attach sponges, algae, hydroids, small soft-coral pieces, shells, and rubble to its body as living camouflage. In a reef aquarium this behavior is part of the appeal, but it also comes with a well-known caveat: hungry or actively redecorating individuals have been reported to snip frags of zoanthids, mushrooms, and other soft corals to wear them. Because of this, the species is generally rated reef-safe with caution rather than fully reef-safe. Most keepers find the frag-theft risk drops noticeably when the crab is well-fed and given access to a varied mix of macroalgae, rubble, and inexpensive sponges to decorate with. The Decorator Crab is strictly nocturnal — it tends to spend the day motionless under an overhang or tucked in a crevice and begins foraging only after the lights go out. Housing it singly is generally advisable; conspecifics tend to be territorial and may fight, particularly around molting periods. Like all marine crustaceans, it is highly sensitive to copper and other heavy metals, so exposure to copper-based parasite medications must be avoided. Iodine and trace-element supplementation is commonly recommended to support healthy molting. Some retailers still list the species under the older family name Majidae; modern crab taxonomy places Camposcia retusa in the family Inachidae within the superfamily Majoidea.
breedingNotes: The Decorator Crab is not known to have been successfully bred in home aquariums. Sex differences become apparent after the crab reaches sexual maturity and are visible in the pleopods (the small abdominal appendages), which differ in number and form between males and females. Females reportedly carry only a few dozen eggs per clutch, with an incubation period of around 21 to 25 days; the resulting larvae pass through several free-swimming planktonic stages that have not been reared in captivity. Aquarium specimens are wild-caught.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — confirmed Camposcia retusa, family listed as Majidae (older placement; modern is Inachidae), care level Easy, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, carnivore (diet leaning meaty), 4-inch max, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, calcium/magnesium/iodine/trace supplements recommended. Designated URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/ root returned 404 on direct product path; product page found via web search at /products/spider-decorator-crab.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Used as second source because BlueZoo Aquatics did not have a Camposcia retusa product page (root domain crawl returned no match). Cross-confirmed: family Majidae (older), order Decapoda, Indo-West Pacific range with country list (Philippines, Japan, India, Sri Lanka, Australia, Fiji), carapace 1.5–2 cm / leg span 8–10 cm, lifespan 3–5 years, min 10 gal / 40 L, temp 78–86°F, pH 8.1–8.5, dKH 8–12, SG 1.021–1.030, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, omnivorous scavenger diet, semi-aggressive, nocturnal velcro-carapace behavior, captive breeding not documented.

additional.name: Reeflings species profile
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed adult size 10 cm, min tank 100 L (26 gal), temp 24–27°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.023–1.025, omnivorous scavenger diet, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with caution (may snip coral pieces for decoration), advised to house singly. Lists family as Inachidae — the modern correct placement used for this entry.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed 10 cm adult, 100 L minimum, ~26 °C, SG 1.026, nocturnal behavior, reef-safe with caution (will sometimes cut at soft corals to decorate themselves and may eat them). Range listed as East Indian Ocean / Indonesia.

crustacean.moltingNotes: The Decorator Crab molts periodically as it grows, shedding its old exoskeleton — a process called exuviation — to allow for growth beneath. Adequate iodine and trace-element levels are commonly considered important for successful molting, as with other marine crustaceans. Discarded exoskeletons are usually left undisturbed in the tank and may be re-consumed by the crab as a source of calcium. Specific intermolt-period data for Camposcia retusa is not well documented in hobby sources. Individuals typically shed their accumulated decorations along with the old shell and re-decorate their new exoskeleton over the following days.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Not known to have been bred in home aquariums; larval phase has not been reared in the hobby.
